strands.title: Replay Module Architecture & Requirements
strands.content: A new offline simulation module is being designed at src/pichay/replay.py to measure the effectiveness of context paging by replaying historical interactions. The module must parse proxy JSONL logs, reconstruct message arrays as they existed at each API call, apply pager.py compaction logic to snapshots, detect page faults (cases where evicted content was re-requested), and report metrics (fault rate, bytes saved, reduction percentage). The module exposes a CLI interface via argparse accepting log file paths, configurable thresholds (--age-threshold default 4, --min-size default 500), and --json for structured output. **CRITICAL STATUS CHANGE: The PRE-IMPLEMENTATION READING PHASE has now entered CYCLE 4 with REPEATED TOOL USE SIGNAL ('[tool_use: Read]') BUT STILL NO EMPIRICAL FINDINGS DELIVERED.** The assistant has now signaled reading intent twice (cycle 3 and cycle 4) without producing code inspection results in conversation. This represents either: (a) a systematic barrier preventing code access/delivery (e.g., codebase not accessible, context constraints), (b) misunderstanding of what was requested (reading was signaled but findings were not meant to be returned conversationally), or (c) a loop/timeout issue. The architecture requirements remain informed by specification rather than codebase introspection. **The conversation is at critical risk of stagnation.** Continued tool-use signaling without deliverables indicates a need for explicit status clarification or alternative approach.

key_claims.text: The replay module must parse proxy JSONL logs and reconstruct message arrays at each API call turn
Paging effectiveness is measurable through offline replay — comparing actual next actions against simulated evictions reveals page faults
Key metrics are fault rate, bytes saved, and reduction percentage, reported per-session and in aggregate
CLI interface requires --age-threshold (default 4), --min-size (default 500), --json flag, and accepts multiple log file paths
Pre-implementation code reading is mandatory to ground design in actual codebase structure

strands.title: Reading Phase Status & Temporal State: CRISIS PATTERN EMERGING
strands.content: **NEW OBSERVATION IN CYCLE 4**: The assistant has now emitted the '[tool_use: Read]' signal TWICE (end of cycle 3 AND cycle 4) without any actual code inspection findings appearing in the conversation. This is a **STATEFUL PATTERN**, not a one-time ambiguity. Possible interpretations: (1) **Tool execution not returning conversational output**: The assistant may be calling a read function, but the results are not being integrated into the response text. This would indicate a gap between tool invocation and delivery of findings to the tensor. (2) **Misaligned workflow**: The assistant may believe that signaling 'Read' is sufficient, without understanding that findings must be explicitly reported back. (3) **Blocking condition**: File access, context limits, or API constraints may be preventing the reading task, but the assistant is not reporting this failure—only the intent. (4) **Stalling behavior**: The repeated signal without progress may indicate a logical loop or timeout in the assistant's reasoning. **DECISION POINT**: Cycle 4 must either (a) escalate to explicit human inquiry about reading status, or (b) abandon the blocked reading approach and proceed with specification-only reasoning. Continuing to wait for tool output that does not arrive in conversation is unsustainable.

key_claims.text: The assistant has signaled reading intent in BOTH cycle 3 and cycle 4
No actual code inspection findings (signatures, schemas, implementation details) have appeared in conversation across either cycle
This repeated pattern (intent signal without deliverable output) indicates a systematic issue, not random omission
The blocking condition is not clearly communicated; the assistant reports 'Read' but not 'Cannot read' or 'Reading blocked'
Proceeding with specification-only reasoning is the only viable path if reading cannot be empirically grounded

strands.title: Dependency Analysis & Pre-Implementation Requirements: UNRESOLVED
strands.content: Three core dependencies persist as unknowns, carrying forward from cycles 2–4. (1) **pager.py compaction logic**: Signature, parameters, return structure, eviction representation remain unobserved. The assistant has signaled intent to read this module twice but has not delivered findings. (2) **proxy.py JSONL schema**: Field names, data types, nesting, session/call/action encoding remain unknown. (3) **eval.py evaluation patterns**: Structure, metrics computation, reporting conventions remain unexamined. **STATUS**: All three reading tasks have been prescribed twice but show no empirical integration. The tensor cannot assume any implementation details and must either: (a) request explicit human confirmation of codebase structure (preferred), or (b) proceed with abstract/pseudocode design that does not depend on specific implementation signatures. All prior open questions remain open, now carrying an additional layer of epistemic uncertainty: not just 'what is the code?' but 'can the code be accessed at all?'

key_claims.text: pager.py contains the compaction logic that replay.py will invoke on reconstructed snapshots
proxy.py defines the JSONL log format that replay.py must parse (schema unknown despite reading intent)
The specific structure, signatures, and mechanics of these modules remain unknown after two cycles of prescribed reading
Either the code is not accessible, or the reading tool is not returning findings to the conversation

strands.title: Core Simulation Logic: Snapshot Reconstruction & Fault Detection
strands.content: The replay simulation architecture remains conceptual and cannot be concretized pending delivery of code inspection findings. For each turn in a session, the module will: (1) reconstruct the messages array as it existed before that API call, (2) apply pager compaction logic with configured thresholds, (3) check whether the model's actual next action references any content that was evicted. A match between evicted content and a subsequent request constitutes a page fault. This logic requires careful state tracking across turns within a session, but the precise mechanisms depend on: (a) how pager.py represents eviction (unknown), (b) how proxy.py encodes the next action and prior messages (unknown), (c) how to reliably match re-requests to evictions (unknown). **CURRENT TRAJECTORY**: The simulation remains at the level of abstract pseudocode. Without empirical grounding, the implementation will require defensive coding assumptions or will risk misalignment with actual codebase. Any actual coding phase should be preceded by explicit codebase walkthrough with confirmed output.

key_claims.text: Snapshot reconstruction requires tracking message array state before each API call turn
A page fault occurs when the model requests content that was evicted by pager compaction in the prior turn
The exact mechanism of fault detection, eviction representation, and re-request matching remains ungrounded
Proceeding to coding without codebase inspection risks implementation misalignment with actual signatures and schemas

strands.title: Metrics & Reporting
strands.content: Two levels of metrics are required. Per-session metrics include fault count, fault rate (faults / total turns), total bytes saved, and reduction percentage (bytes saved / original size). Aggregate metrics roll these up across all sessions in the input. The --json flag triggers machine-readable output (JSON format) rather than human-readable text. The module must track both the state before and after compaction to compute bytes saved and reduction percentage accurately. The precise output format (both text and JSON) awaits grounding by examination of eval.py reporting patterns and conventions, which have not been delivered despite prescribed reading. All statements remain prescriptive rather than empirically derived. **RISK**: Without reference implementation from eval.py, output formatting may not align with project conventions, leading to post-implementation refactoring.

key_claims.text: Per-session metrics: fault count, fault rate, bytes saved, reduction percentage
Aggregate metrics are computed across all sessions in the input
--json flag enables machine-readable output (JSON format)
Output formatting patterns remain ungrounded pending examination of eval.py (not yet delivered)

strands.title: Pre-Implementation Reading Workflow Status: BLOCKED OR STALLED
strands.content: **ESCALATION REQUIRED**: The reading workflow has now entered a SECOND CYCLE of intent signaling without deliverables. Cycle 3 ended with '[tool_use: Read]'; cycle 4 repeats the same signal. This is not ambiguity—this is a **SYSTEMATIC FAILURE TO DELIVER EMPIRICAL FINDINGS**. The conversation shows no evidence that: (a) pager.py has been inspected and findings reported, (b) proxy.py schema has been extracted and documented, (c) eval.py patterns have been identified. The scaffolding phase is complete. The reading phase is blocked or not returning output. The empirical grounding phase cannot begin. **DECISION REQUIRED**: The next cycle must either: (1) **ESCALATE**: Explicitly ask whether code files are accessible and request a direct response about reading feasibility (not another tool signal), or (2) **PIVOT**: Abandon the blocked reading task and proceed with specification-only design, using best-guess assumptions about codebase structure, clearly marked as ASSUMPTIONS rather than observations. Continuing to signal 'Read' without receiving findings will cause indefinite stagnation. All post-reading deliverables (data flow diagram, concrete data structures, error handling, CLI design, coding checklist) remain unmade.

key_claims.text: Reading pager.py was prescribed with explicit priority; tool signal appeared twice without findings
Reading proxy.py was prescribed with explicit priority; tool signal appeared twice without findings
The repeated '[tool_use: Read]' signal without conversational output indicates a gap between tool invocation and delivery
Stalling on reading will prevent all downstream work (specification, design, implementation)
Explicit escalation (request for feasibility clarification) or pivot to assumption-based design is necessary to unblock
